title: Hydrophobic Membranes as Liquid Junction-Free Reference Electrodes
abstract:
Hydrophobic Membranes as Liquid Junction-Free Reference
Electrodes
Eric Bakker*
Department of Chemistry, Auburn University, Auburn, AL 36849, USA
Received: November 12, 1998
Final version: January 7, 1999
Abstract
This article critically compares four recently proposed chemical designs to develop miniaturizable reference electrodes on the basis of
hydrophobic membrane materials. The use of such materials would be highly promising to develop potentiometric sensor arrays for the
assessment of a multitude of ionic analytes since manufacturing the reference electrode would not be substantially different from the actual
sensing electrodes. Results and possible pitfalls in the proposed design schemes are discussed. It is shown that the use of an anion and
cation responsive membrane, connected in parallel, will only function under extremely well-de®ned conditions. The use of essentially
nonresponsive polyurethane materials has shown considerable worth, but the results could currently also be explained with a porous
membrane that acts as a liquid junction. Using inert electrolytes in the membrane with well-tuned lipophilicity, on the other hand, shows
usefulness but requires very careful experimental and theoretical characterization. Polyion responsive membranes are an additional, very
different approach to developing reference electrodes, especially in view of an application in whole blood samples.
Keywords: Hydrophobic membranes, Liquid junction-free reference electrodes, Miniaturizable
1. Introduction
In the past years, potentiometric membrane electrodes have
been successfully miniaturized into screen printed ion sensing
arrays for clinical and biomedical applications [1]. A reference
electrode is always required to complete the electrochemical
measuring cell. Traditionally, reference electrodes contain a
classical reference element such as Ag=AgCl and a rather volu-
minous aqueous bridge electrolyte that is in contact with the
sample via a liquid junction. The in¯uence of different sample
compositions on the liquid junction potential can be kept small
and calculated to some extent on the basis of the Henderson [2]
or Planck formalisms [3]. Unfortunately, such reference elec-
trodes have always been relatively cumbersome to use and
maintain, and they are extremely dif®cult to miniaturize. With
voltammetric or amperometric techniques, the tolerated uncer-
tainty in the potential is still relatively large since the analyte
concentration is related primarily to the measured current, which
is quite constant within a certain potential window. A silver=
silver chloride element coated with a microporous material such
as Na®on has been successfully implemented in voltammetric
systems [4]. With potentiometric sensors, however, the zero-
current potential is directly related to the analyte ion activity. The
potential change at the reference electrode as a function of the
sample composition must therefore be kept reliably small. The
group of Buck has used a silver=silver chloride element covered
with a nonionic hydrogel polymer as an initial reference electrode
to determine pH and potassium ion ¯uctuations in beating hearts
with their miniaturized sensing arrays on Kapton as polymeric
support [1]. While the hydrogel provides some protection against
large biomolecules, it is well established that the silver=silver
chloride element will be sensitive to concentration ¯uctuations of
chloride, thiocyanate, and sulfur containing amino acids in the
sample [5]. Other researchers have offered different, often more
convincing reference electrode concepts based on liquid junc-
tions [4, 6±8].
For reasons of ease of fabrication, miniaturized reference
electrodes for use in potentiometric sensor arrays would ideally
be manufactured from the same materials as the sensor mem-
branes themselves, and consist of hydrophobic phases such as
plasticized
poly(vinyl
chloride),
silicone
rubber,
or
poly-
(urethane). In recent years, a number of such systems have been
proposed in the literature [9±13]. Unfortunately, these concepts
have not yet been critically discussed and compared in terms of
their response mechanism, possible limitations, and practical
feasibility. This article performs a critical comparison of four
recently introduced reference electrode systems on the basis of
hydrophobic membranes. Their response mechanism and their
applicability as successful reference electrode concepts is eval-
uated.
2. Results and Discussion
The four reference electrode concepts discussed in this article
all rely on hydrophobic membrane materials. These materials are
basically identical to the ones that are used to fabricate ion
responsive sensors. Consequently, they share a common response
behavior toward small sample ions. It is well-established [14] that
a hydrophobic membrane material in contact with an aqueous
sample solution responds according the phase boundary potential
EPB:
EPB  RT

where aI is the activity of the ion with charge zI in the sample
phase boundary, gI, and [IzI] are the activity coef®cient and the
concentration of the so-called free ion IZI in the organic mem-
brane phase boundary, and R, T, and F are the gas constant, the
absolute temperature, and the Faraday constant. The constant kI
incorporates the free energy of transfer of the ion IZI, i.e.,
kI  expfm0aqÿm0orgg=RT), where m is the standard chem-
ical potential for the ion in the indicated phase. Equation 1 is
equally valid for anions, in which case zI assumes a negative
value.

# WILEY-VCH Verlag GmbH, D-69469 Weinheim, 1999
1040±0397/99/1007±0788 $17.50:50=0
788
 While Equation 1 describes potential changes at the very phase
boundary, the relevant ion partitioning processes may be dictated
by thermodynamic as well as kinetic processes via ion ¯uxes
across the membrane. Generally, Equation 1 demonstrates the
dif®culty to develop hydrophobic membranes for use as reference
electrodes. Such membranes extract only minute amounts of
hydrophilic electrolytes if no ionophore is present as additional
driving force for ion extraction. A membrane with a net con-
centration of a lipophilic ion exchanger on the order of 50 mM
[15], therefore, will lead to a substantial potential change as a
function of the sample composition (since [IzI] in Equation 1 is
then approximately constant). This is the basic response
mechanism of ion-selective membrane electrodes. If no ion-
exchanger at all would be present, Equation 1 expects a pro-
portionality between the ion activity in the sample and in the
membrane. For one given electrolyte, therefore, EPB would be
activity-independent [16]. However, every ion possesses a dif-
ferent free energy of transfer between the sample and organic
phase, symbolized by kI in Equation 1. A different electrolyte
would therefore lead to a different EPB value, and a mixture of
electrolytes would give an intermediate potential. These theoret-
ical considerations make it seemingly quite dif®cult to devise
novel, workable reference electrode concepts on the basis of such
membrane materials. Nonetheless, four recent articles have
claimed that this is indeed feasible. These contributions and their
possible working mechanisms will be discussed further in detail.
2.1. Nagy's Approach: Using two ISE Membranes in
Parallel
Nagy and co-workers [9] have proposed two ion-selective
electrode membranes connected in parallel to be used as refer-
ence electrode (Fig. 1). Both membranes are composed of plas-
ticized PVC and additional copolymers, while one is doped with
potassium tetrakis(4-chlorophenyl)borate which renders it cation
responsive, and the other with tridodecylmethylammonium
chloride, which shows anion exchanger properties. If both elec-
trode membranes are connected in parallel via a resistor, and both
intrinsically respond to monovalent cations and anions in solution
according to the Nernst equation, the EMF resulting from the
combined membrane electrode is seemingly independent of
sample concentration changes and could be used as a reference
electrode. The concept has been put to practice with a commer-
cial Fÿ responsive electrode for ¯uoride measurements in various
NaF sample concentrations.
This approach has a number of shortcomings that have not yet
been discussed in the literature. Perhaps most importantly, each
of the two hydrophobic membranes intrinsically responds dif-
ferently to different sample ions (i.e., kI in Eq. 1 varies for every
ion). In analogy to a membrane without ion-exchanger properties
(see discussion above), the measured potential will change as the
nature of the sample electrolyte varies. Since such ion-exchanger
membranes discriminate among different ions by sometimes
many orders of magnitude, this may account to several hundred
millivolt undesired potential change. Similarly, widely varying
intermediate potentials are expected as mixtures of different ions
are present in the sample. Since this is invariably the case with
real world samples, the concept is ill-fated for general use. One
solution to this problem would be to develop membrane materials
that are equally responsive to all ions of interest. This would
likely involve doping the membrane with suitable ionophores that
complex different sample ions to the extent that they exactly
counterbalance their intrinsic differences in the free energy of
transfer. Finding a combination of ionophores to accomplish this
task is extremely challenging. Moreover, if multivalent ions are
present in solution the concept will invariably fail because higher
charges lead to decreasing electrode slopes. A different, prob-
lematic aspect of this concept is that electrolyte can freely diffuse
across both membranes and dilute or concentrate the inner ®lling
solutions. This is possible because both membranes are in elec-
trical contact with each other by a simple resistor which allows a
local current to ¯ow. Upon every sample change, therefore,
signi®cant ion ¯uxes may be observed that may limit the func-
tionality of the setup in some situations. This is especially critical
in miniaturized applications, where only a very small volume of
inner electrolyte can be tolerated.
2.2. Cha's Approach: Making Nonresponsive Aromatic
Polyurethanes
Very recently, Nam, Cha and co-workers have exploited the
fact that various polyurethane membrane materials for use in ion-
selective electrodes were found to be essentially nonresponsive
[10]. While this was a disappointing experience in sensor
development, the authors have realized that such materials may
have considerable promise for use in junction-free reference
electrodes. Consequently, they reevaluated and optimized a
number of previously unsuccessful materials for use as reference
electrode membranes and found that a certain class of aromatic
polyurethanes was especially promising in this respect. Interest-
ingly, doping the membrane with various salts such as ETH 500
or a manual 1 : 1 mixture of potassium tetrakis(4-chloro-
phenyl)borate and tridodecylmethylammonium chloride or tet-
rakis[tris(dimethylamino)phosphoranylidenamino]phosphonium
chloride showed effectively the same nonresponsive behavior as
an undoped membrane. For optimum functioning, the authors
Fig. 1. Schematic presentation of using a cation and an anion responsive
membrane connected in parallel to fabricate a reference electrode [9].
This concept is only useful in extremely specialized cases.

(see discussion above), the measured potential will change as the
nature of the sample electrolyte varies. Since such ion-exchanger
membranes discriminate among different ions by sometimes
many orders of magnitude, this may account to several hundred
millivolt undesired potential change. Similarly, widely varying
intermediate potentials are expected as mixtures of different ions
are present in the sample. Since this is invariably the case with
real world samples, the concept is ill-fated for general use. One

that are equally responsive to all ions of interest. This would

transfer. Finding a combination of ionophores to accomplish this

lematic aspect of this concept is that electrolyte can freely diffuse

tionality of the setup in some situations. This is especially critical
in miniaturized applications, where only a very small volume of
inner electrolyte can be tolerated.
2.2. Cha's Approach: Making Nonresponsive Aromatic
Very recently, Nam, Cha and co-workers have exploited the
fact that various polyurethane membrane materials for use in ion-
selective electrodes were found to be essentially nonresponsive
[10]. While this was a disappointing experience in sensor
development, the authors have realized that such materials may
have considerable promise for use in junction-free reference
electrodes. Consequently, they reevaluated and optimized a
number of previously unsuccessful materials for use as reference
electrode membranes and found that a certain class of aromatic
polyurethanes was especially promising in this respect. Interest-
ingly, doping the membrane with various salts such as ETH 500
or a manual 1 : 1 mixture of potassium tetrakis(4-chloro-
phenyl)borate and tridodecylmethylammonium chloride or tet-
rakis[tris(dimethylamino)phosphoranylidenamino]phosphonium
chloride showed effectively the same nonresponsive behavior as
an undoped membrane. For optimum functioning, the authors
Fig. 1. Schematic presentation of using a cation and an anion responsive
membrane connected in parallel to fabricate a reference electrode [9].
This concept is only useful in extremely specialized cases.
Liquid Junction-Free Reference Electrodes
cured the materials in an oven at elevated temperature (55 C) for
at least 12 h. They mounted the membranes in regular Philips
bodies, back®lled them with 0.1 M KCl, and found no appreci-
able response in a variety of sample electrolytes. A preliminary
miniaturized reference electrode membrane, screen printed onto a
silver metal electrode was also found to work satisfactorily in
samples containing various concentrations of KCl.
At ®rst sight, the system introduced by Nam and Cha seems
extremely promising, and a large amount of experimental data
was collected to extensively evaluate their system. There is little
doubt that their concept is convincing in the con®gurations that
were tested. Nonetheless, the question remains why this class of
material shows apparent non responsive behavior to sample ions.
This was not truly addressed by the authors. Their potential±time
traces seem to be very stable, and there must be a well-de®ned
underlying mechanism involved according to which of these
systems function so well. Again, hydrophobic membranes typi-
cally respond according to Equation 1. Doping the membrane
with different salts and ionophores would normally invariably
lead to large differences in response behavior. The behavior of
this system is therefore most readily understood if the membrane
material would in fact be microporous and act as liquid junction
between the 0.1 M KCl inner electrolyte and the sample. Other
researchers have indeed used polyurethanes instead of Na®on for
building reference electrode membranes for use in amperometric
sensors, with seemingly the same physicochemical properties [4].
It is well known that Na®on is a microporous polymeric material.
It would be highly interesting to evaluate Nam and Cha's results
with other, nonequitransferent electrolytes on the backside of the
membrane. While more experiments would be needed to fully
evaluate the response mechanism of these types of reference
electrodes, it seems currently most likely that they act as poly-
meric liquid junctions. If this is the case, it would likely make
them unsuitable for use in miniaturized sensor systems.
2.3. Horvai's Approach: Doping the Membrane with an
Horvai has evaluated traditional plasticized PVC membranes
that were doped with various lipophilic electrolytes, e.g., salts
of the general class tetraalkylammonium tetrakis(4-chloro-
phenyl)borate for use as reference electrodes [11, 12]. He found
that some formulations showed very small potential ¯uctuations
as a function of the sample composition. Although an extensive
set of experimental data was not presented in the literature, this
interesting contribution differs from the others in its proposed
mechanism. Ordinarily, the lipophilic electrolyte is assumed to be
con®ned to the membrane phase. Its main role is to decrease the
membrane resistance and the activity coef®cient of their coun-
terions, which may be bene®cial to selectivity [17]. However, as
the lipophilicity of the electrolyte is decreased, its partitioning
into the aqueous phase may become signi®cant. One can in
principle distinguish three distinct situations involving these
lipophilic electrolytes: 1) at very high lipophilicity of anion and
cation of the inert electrolyte, the extraction of sample ions into
the membrane is potential determining. In this case, the mem-
brane responds according to Equation 1, where IzI is any sample
ion. If the membrane has no ion-exchanger properties, again, the
response is independent of the sample ion activity but dependent
on the chemical nature of the sample electrolyte [16]. 2) at
intermediate lipophilicity of the inert electrolyte, the anion and
cation may signi®cantly partition into the aqueous phase and
become potential determining (Fig. 2). The membrane would
now still respond to Equation 1, but IzI is now any of the two
partitioning membrane ions. In this mode, the membrane may
function as a reference electrode since the potential is dictated by
membrane ions, relatively independent of the sample composi-
tion. This explanation is analogous to the explained origin of the
detection limit of ISEs by a leaching process of potential deter-
mining ions from the membrane [18]. 3) At low lipophilicity of
the inert electrolyte, the anion and cation will rapidly and irre-
versibly leach out of the membrane. This loss will soon make the
membrane responsive to sample composition changes in analogy
In addition to the three situations described above where
coextraction effects are dominant, one has to consider ion-
exchange effects between membrane and sample ions of the same
charge. If one membrane ion exchanges with a sample ion while
the counterion does not, the membrane will start to exhibit
undesirable ion-exchange properties. Since this process will
exchanging sample ion, it must be prohibited if the membrane is
to be used as a reference electrode. This shows that the concept
of using a membrane doped with a relatively lipophilic electro-
lyte is feasible, but requires thoughtful planning and character-
ization. One principal drawback of the idea is that unknown
samples containing lipophilic ions may disturb the system, and
that the lifetime of the membrane electrode may be limited by the
partitioning process of the lipophilic electrolyte.
2.4. Bakker's Approach: Using Polyion-Responsive
A very different direction to achieve a liquid junction free
reference electrode has recently been proposed by Bakker and co-
workers [13]. It extends interesting concepts that have been
introduced with the advent of potentiometric membrane elec-
trodes that are responsive to polyionic analytes [19]. The main
Fig. 2. The lipophilicity of an inert electrolyte R1 R2ÿ embedded in a
hydrophobic membrane may be tailored to obtain a response that is
independent of common sample electrolytes [11, 12]. This idea may be
workable for certain applications.
principle is simple to understand. If a membrane electrode can be
made responsive to a highly charged analyte, the resulting
Nernstian response function will exhibit a very small electrode
slope that is inversely proportional to the charge of the analyte. If
some variable amount of this analyte is continuously present at
the membrane surface, the resulting potential will be nearly
independent of its concentration (Fig. 3). It must be emphasized
that the polyion sensors developed so far showed high apparent
electrode slopes in the time frame of practical measurements.
This effect has been thoroughly characterized and is due to
incomplete exchange of the initial monovalent ion (typically
chloride) from the membrane phase boundary upon extraction of
the polyionic analyte [20]. The extraction process is initially
diffusion limited, i.e., extracted polyions diffuse rapidly away
from the phase boundary region into the bulk of the membrane,
making it dif®cult to expel all remaining monovalent ions from
the membrane phase. This interesting response mechanism has
made it possible to design measuring protocols that allow one to
use the polyion sensor in a nonclassical fashion. Repetitive
stripping of the polyion from the membrane between single
measurements is necessary for increased accuracy and to elim-
Since some well-established anticoagulants (such as heparin
and liquoid) are polyions, and membrane electrodes have been
speci®cally designed to measure such anticoagulants in blood,
it seems feasible to design reference electrode membranes for
use in blood samples. For this to work, the polyion must have
occupied all available ion exchanger sites of the membrane, i.e.,
suf®cient exposure time to polyion solutions must be allowed. It
has been shown that polyion diffusion across polymeric mem-
branes is signi®cantly slower than that of typical monovalent
ions. For example, up to 24 hours have been necessary to reach
the thermodynamically expected potential value upon introduc-
tion of moderate heparin levels to the sample [20, 22]. To reach a
state of complete equilibration, therefore, long conditioning times
of several days to one week must be allowed.
As Figure 3 shows, the regular chloride ion response function
of a plasticized PVC membrane containing the ion-exchanger
tridodecylmethylammonium chloride is completely suppressed
after conditioning the membrane for prolonged periods of time in
a solution containing the anticoagulant poly(anethole sulfonate)
(liquoid) [13]. Evidently, a concentration-independent response
was obtained even in solutions containing no anticoagulant (Fig.
3). Nonetheless, this direction is primarily targeted to samples
that contain a suitable polyionic analyte, such as whole blood.
The primary disadvantage of this concept is that highly lipophilic
ions may ion-exchange with the polyion in the membrane,
thereby restoring its response to small ions. Therefore, careful
control of conditioning and measuring conditions must be
accomplished for a successful application of this interesting class
Developing miniaturizable reference electrodes on the basis of
hydrophobic membranes is a challenging task that is of high
practical use to make truly useful integrated potentiometric sen-
sing arrays. Several interesting schemes have been reported but
after careful consideration of their response mechanism, all have
their promises and shortcomings. This discussion has revealed
that a universal reference electrode system is likely not feasible
on the basis of such membranes. Rather, the appropriate mem-
brane chemistry needs to be optimized for a certain set of target
applications. Most important, the assumptions that warrant a
reliable functioning of such electrodes need to be carefully
reviewed before putting the concept to practice. The quest of
every researcher to make a system workable should not distract
one from the theoretical working principles and practical short-
comings that accompany any novel reference electrode principle.

[5] W.E. Morf, G. Kahr, W. Simon, Anal. Chem. 1974, 46, 1538.
[6] D. Diamond, E. Mcenroe, M. Mccarrick, A. Lewenstam, Electro-
ronskaptasinska, E. Sudholter, D.N. Reinhoudt, Sens. Actuators

suppressed after all membrane chloride ions have been replaced by the highly charged anion poly(anethole sulfonate) (right) [13]. Such a con®guration
may be useful as a reference electrode in well-de®ned situations.
